With what he had in his soul, he felt sore and uncomfortable in the restaurant, in the midst of private rooms where men were dining with ladies, in all this fuss and bustle; the surroundings of bronzes, looking glasses, gas, and waiters-all of it was offensive to him. He was afraid of sullying what his soul was brimful of.
"I? Yes, I am; but besides, all this bothers me," he said. "You can't conceive how queer it all seems to a country person like me, as queer as that gentleman's nails I saw at your place..."
"Yes, I saw how much interested you were in poor Grinevitch's nails," said Stepan Arkadyevitch, laughing.

"It's too much for me," responded Levin. "Do try, now, and put yourself in my place, take the point of view of a country person. We in the country try to bring our hands into such a state as will be most convenient for working with. So we cut our nails; sometimes we turn up our sleeves. And here people purposely let their nails grow as long as they will, and link on small saucers by way of studs, so that they can do nothing with their hands."
Stepan Arkadyevitch smiled gaily.
"Oh, yes, that's just a sign that he has no need to do coarse work. His work is with the mind..."

"Maybe. But still it's queer to me, just as at this moment it seems queer to me that we country folks try to get our meals over as soon as we can, so as to be ready for our work, while here are we trying to drag out our meal as long as possible, and with that object eating oysters..."
"Why, of course," objected Stepan Arkadyevitch. "But that's just the aim of civilization-to make everything a source of enjoyment."
"Well, if that's its aim, I'd rather be a savage."
"And so you are a savage. All you Levins are savages."
Levin sighed. He remembered his brother Nikolay, and felt ashamed and sore, and he scowled; but Oblonsky began speaking of a subject which at once drew his attention.
"Oh, I say, are you going tonight to our people, the Shtcherbatskys', I mean?" he said, his eyes sparkling significantly as he pushed away the empty rough shells, and drew the cheese towards him.
"Yes, I shall certainly go," replied Levin; "though I fancied the princess was not very warm in her invitation."

"What nonsense! That's her manner.... Come, boy, the soup!.... That's her manner-grande dame," said Stepan Arkadyevitch. "I'm coming there too, but I have to go to the Countess Bonina's rehearsal. Only consider, what a savage you are! How do you explain your leaving Moscow so suddenly? The Shtcherbatskys were asking me about you, as though I ought to know. The only thing I know is that you always do what no one else does."
"Yes," said Levin, slowly and with emotion, "you're right. I am a savage. Only, my savageness is not in in my having gone away, but in my coming now. Now I have come..."
"Oh, what a lucky fellow you are!" Stepan Arkadyevitch broke in, looking into Levin's eyes.
"Why?"
"I know a fast horse by his gait, and I know a young man in love by his eyes," said Stepan Arkadyevitch. "Everything is before you."
"And for yourself?"
"For me, it's all behind me."
"Why, how's that?"
"Well, I'm not saying exactly so. But the future's yours, and the present's mine, and the present's not all it might be."
"How so?"

"Oh, it's bad. But I don't want to talk of myself, and besides I can't explain it all," said Stepan Arkadyevitch. "Well, why have you come to Moscow, then?.... Hi! take away!" he shouted to the Tatar.
"You guess?" responded Levin, from whom his eyes did not move.
"I guess, but I can't be the first to talk about it. You can see by that whether I guess right or wrong," said Stepan Arkadyevitch, gazing at Levin with a subtle smile.
"Well, and what have you to tell me?" said Levin with a quivering voice, feeling that all the muscles of his face were quivering too. "How do you look at the matter?"
Stepan Arkadyevitch slowly emptied his glass of Chablis, never taking his eyes off Levin.
"I?" said Stepan Arkadyevitch, "I would like nothing better-nothing. It's the best thing that could be."
"But, but... you're not mistaken? You know what we're speaking of?" said Levin, piercing him with his eyes. "You think it's possible?"
"I think it's possible. Why not possible?"
"No, do you really think it's possible? No, tell me all you think! No, if ... if rejection is in store for me!.... I'm certain indeed..."
"Why should you think so?" said Stepan Arkadyevitch, smiling at his excitement.
"It seems so to me sometimes. That will be awful for me, and for her too."
"Oh, no, anyway, there's nothing awful in it for a girl. Every girl's proud of an offer."
"Yes, every girl, but not she."

Stepan Arkadyevitch smiled. He so well knew that feeling of Levin's, that for him all the girls in the world were divided into two classes: one class-all the girls in the world except her, and those girls with all sorts of human imperfections, and very ordinary girls: the other class-she alone, having no imperfections, and higher than all humanity.
"Wait, take some sauce," he said, holding back Levin's hand as it pushed away the sauce.
Levin submissively helped himself to sauce, but would not let Stepan Arkadyevitch go on eating.

"No, wait, wait," he said. "You must understand that it's a question of life and death for me. I have never spoken to any one of this. And I can speak to no one but you. You know we're different in everything: in tastes, in views, in everything; but I know you love me and understand me, and that's why I'm awfully fond of you. But for God's sake, be quite open with me."

"I am telling you what I think," said Stepan Arkadyevitch, smiling. "But I will say more: my wife-a wonderful woman..." Stepan Arkadyevitch sighed, remembering his relations with his wife, and after a moment's silence resumed-"She has a gift of foreseeing things. She sees through people; but that's not all, she knows what will come to pass, especially in the way of marriages. She foretold, for instance, that Natalia would marry Lvov. Well, she says that Kitty will be your wife."
"Does she?" said Levin.
"She does. Not only that, but she has seen it for ever so long. And she says that you will make her a good husband."
"Oh, if you only knew how such words encourage me!.... But tell me, do you see nothing... well, against it?"
"Against it? Not a single thing."

"No, there is something," said Levin. "I've no career, and I'm a country gentleman. That will be a drawback in her family's eyes, and then... but wait, you must know everything. I'm thirty-two, and she's only eighteen. She's a child. There's something... there's a difference in age... it's a big difference... and then, I've been through a lot, I've... well, I'm not what I was when I was a boy... but wait, I've got to tell you one thing more: I'm not a person who... I'm not... well, I've got to say it... I'm not a pure person. I've lived... well, as men do live."

"My dear boy, you're making a mountain out of a molehill," said Stepan Arkadyevitch, laughing. "You've lived as men live. Well, what of that? All men of your age and position have lived as men live. Why, you've not been doing anything out of the way! You're just a man, like any other."
"No, it's not that," said Levin, "it's that... well, you must understand, I want to be honest. I want to tell her everything."

"Oh, God forbid!" cried Stepan Arkadyevitch. "Don't you ever do anything of the kind! That's a great mistake. Women don't understand that sort of honesty. They like to have a man who's a bit of a mystery, a man who's been around. If you tell her all that, she'll only be disgusted. No, no, keep it all to yourself."
Levin shook his head.
"I can't do that," he said. "I must be honest with her. If she's to be my wife, she must know everything. I couldn't bear to have any secrets from her."
"Well, do as you like, but I tell you it's a mistake. But anyway, let's talk of something else. Have you seen Vronsky?"
"Vronsky? No, I haven't. Who is he?"

"He's a young officer, very wealthy, and very much the fashion just now. He's been in Moscow for some time, and he's often at the Shtcherbatskys'. Between you and me, I think the Princess is rather keen on him for Kitty."
Levin felt a pang of jealousy.
"Is he?.... And what does Kitty think of him?"

"I don't know. She's always very friendly with him, but that's her way. She's friendly with everyone. But I tell you what: you come to the Shtcherbatskys' tonight, and you'll see for yourself. Vronsky's sure to be there."

Levin felt a sense of apprehension. He knew that Vronsky was a dangerous rival, and he felt that his chances of winning Kitty were even smaller than he had thought. But he also felt a sense of determination. He would go to the Shtcherbatskys', and he would tell Kitty how he felt. Whatever happened, he would know the truth.
They finished their dinner and left the restaurant. As they walked along the street, Levin felt a sense of excitement and fear. He knew that this evening would decide his fate.
Levin emptied his glass, and they were silent for a while. "There's one other thing I ought to tell you. Do you know Vronsky?" Stepan Arkadyevitch asked Levin.
"No, I don't. Why do you ask?"
"Give us another bottle," Stepan Arkadyevitch directed the Tatar, who was filling up their glasses and fidgeting round them just when he was not wanted.
"Why you ought to know Vronsky is that he's one of your rivals."
"Who's Vronsky?" said Levin, and his face was suddenly transformed from the look of childlike ecstasy which Oblonsky had just been admiring to an
expression of anger and spite.

"Vronsky is one of the sons of Count Kirill Ivanovitch Vronsky, and one of the finest specimens of the gilded youth of Petersburg. I made his acquaintance in Tver when I was on duty there, and he came there for the levy of recruits. Fearfully rich, handsome, great connections, an aide-de-camp, and withal a very nice, good-natured fellow. But he's more than merely a good-natured fellow; as I've come to know him here, he's a man of education and of intellect; he's a man who'll make a fortune."
Levin scowled and was silent.
"Well, he turned up here soon after you'd gone, and as far as I can see, he's over head and ears in love with Kitty; and you know that her mother..."
"Excuse me, but I know nothing," said Levin, gloomily knitting his brows. And immediately he thought of his brother Nikolay and how mean he was to have been able to forget him.

"You wait a minute, wait a minute," said Stepan Arkadyevitch, smiling and touching his hand. "I've told you what I know, and I repeat that in this delicate and awful matter, as far as one can conjecture, I believe the chances are in your favor."
Levin fell back in his chair; his face was pale.
"But what is it?"
"I should advise you to settle the thing as soon as may be," said Oblonsky, getting up from the table.
"Oh, no, thank you! I can't make an offer in the street," said Levin.
"Why not? Today at the skating-ground, and then tonight at the Shtcherbatskys'."
"No, that's impossible."
"Well, anyway, we'll see each other at dinner."
"Very well," said Levin, and he went out of the restaurant, feeling that his heart was beating with such violence that he could hear it.

When Vronsky saw Levin, he was struck by the strange, new expression on his face. Levin was looking at him with a look of hatred and defiance, and Vronsky could not understand why. He had never seen Levin before, and he had no idea who he was. But he felt that there was something between them, and he was curious to know what it was.
He went up to Oblonsky and asked him who the man was.
"That's Levin," said Oblonsky. "A very old friend of mine. He's just come from the country."
"And why does he look at me like that?" asked Vronsky.
Oblonsky laughed.
"Oh, that's just his way. He's a bit of a savage, you know. But he's a very good fellow, really."

Vronsky was not satisfied with this explanation. He felt that there was something more to it than that. He watched Levin as he skated away, and he felt a sense of unease. He knew that Levin was a man to be reckoned with, and he felt that he had a rival for Kitty's hand.

He skated over to Kitty and began to talk to her. He was his usual charming and witty self, and Kitty seemed to be enjoying his company. But Vronsky could see that she was distracted, and that her eyes were constantly following Levin.
"Who is that man?" she asked Vronsky.
"That's Levin," said Vronsky. "Do you know him?"
"Yes, I know him," said Kitty, and her face became flushed. "He's an old friend of our family."

Vronsky felt a pang of jealousy. He saw the way Kitty looked at Levin, and he knew that she had a great deal of affection for him. He felt that he had to do something to win her over, and he decided to be even more attentive and charming than usual.

He skated with Kitty for a while, and then they went to sit down on a bench. He talked to her about his life in Petersburg, about his friends, and about his plans for the future. He was trying to impress her, and he could see that she was impressed. But he also felt that there was something missing, that he was not reaching her in the way that he wanted to.
"You're very quiet today," he said to her.
"I'm just thinking," she replied.
"About what?"
"About many things."
Vronsky felt that he was losing her, and he decided to make a bold move. He took her hand and looked into her eyes.
"Kitty," he said, "I have something to tell you."
But before he could say anything more, they were interrupted by the arrival of the Princess. She was looking for Kitty, and she was not pleased to see her with Vronsky.
"Kitty, it's time to go," she said. "We have a lot to do before the ball tonight."

Kitty got up and followed her mother to the carriage. Vronsky watched them go, and he felt a sense of frustration. He knew that he had been close to telling Kitty how he felt, and he was angry that he had been interrupted. But he also felt a sense of determination. He would see Kitty tonight at the ball, and he would tell her then.

He went back to his quarters and began to prepare for the ball. He wanted to look his best, and he spent a long time choosing his clothes and grooming himself. He was excited and nervous, and he could not wait for the evening to begin.

At the ball, Vronsky was the center of attention. He was handsome, wealthy, and successful, and everyone wanted to talk to him. He was his usual charming and witty self, and he was enjoying the attention. But he was also looking for Kitty, and he was disappointed when he did not see her.

He spent the evening dancing with different ladies, but he was not enjoying himself. He was thinking of Kitty, and he was wondering why she was not there. He began to feel a sense of unease, and he wondered if something had happened to her.

At last, he saw her. She was standing with her mother and a group of friends. She was wearing a beautiful white dress, and she looked more beautiful than ever. Vronsky felt a surge of joy, and he went over to her at once.
"Kitty, you're here at last!" he said.
Kitty smiled at him, but she looked tired and distracted. "Yes, I'm here," she said.

Vronsky asked her for a dance, and she accepted. As they danced, Vronsky felt that he was in heaven. He was close to her, he could smell her perfume, and he could feel the touch of her hand. He wanted to tell her that he loved her, but he was afraid of being rejected.
"You look beautiful tonight," he said to her.
"Thank you," she replied.
"I've been looking for you all evening."
"I'm sorry, I was delayed."

Vronsky felt that she was being distant, and he wondered why. He saw her looking around the room, and he knew that she was looking for Levin. He felt a pang of jealousy, and he decided to be even more attentive and charming.
"Is anything the matter?" he asked her.
"No, nothing," she replied.
"You seem distracted."
"I'm just a little tired."

Vronsky did not believe her. He knew that there was something else on her mind, and he was determined to find out what it was. He asked her for another dance, but she declined, saying that she wanted to rest. Vronsky was disappointed, but he did not press her. He led her back to her mother and went to talk to some of his friends.

He spent the rest of the evening watching Kitty. He saw her talking to Levin, and he saw the way she looked at him. He felt a sense of despair, and he knew that he was losing her. He saw Levin and Kitty leave the ball together, and he felt a sense of bitterness and resentment. He knew that Levin had won, and he felt that his life was over.

He went back to his quarters and spent the night in a state of misery. He felt that he had been a fool to think that Kitty could ever love him, and he wished that he had never come to Moscow. He decided to leave the city the next day and go back to Petersburg. But in the morning, he changed his mind. He decided to stay and fight for Kitty. He knew that it would not be easy, but he was determined to win her over.

He went to the Shtcherbatskys' house in the afternoon, but he was told that they were not at home. He went back to his quarters and spent the rest of the day in a state of agitation and uncertainty. He was thinking of Kitty, and he was wondering what she was doing. He was also thinking of Levin, and he was wondering what he was doing.

In the evening, he went to the theater, hoping to see Kitty there. He saw her in a box with her mother and Levin. He watched them from his seat in the stalls, and he felt a sense of anger and jealousy. He saw Levin and Kitty talking and laughing, and he felt that they were happy together. He felt that he was being ignored and forgotten, and he wished that he could do something to stop it.

When the performance was over, he went to the entrance to wait for the Shtcherbatskys. He saw them come out and get into their carriage. He went up to them and spoke to the Princess, but she was cold and distant. She told him that they were busy and could not see him. Vronsky felt a sense of humiliation and defeat. He realized that the Princess did not want him as a son-in-law, and that she was encouraging Levin's suit.

He went back to his quarters and spent the night in a state of deep depression. He felt that he had lost Kitty, and that his life was over. He decided to leave Moscow the next day and go back to Petersburg. This time, he was determined to go. He could not stay in a city where he was not wanted, and where he was being humiliated. He packed his things and left the city on the morning train. As he traveled back to Petersburg, he felt a sense of sadness and regret, but he also felt a sense of relief. He was going back to his own world, where he was respected and admired, and where he could forget about Kitty and Levin.

Stepan Arkadyevitch's visit to his sister was a mission of peace, but it was also an escape from the stifling atmosphere of his own home. He had always been fond of Anna, not only because she was his sister, but because she possessed a certain vitality and charm that he found irresistible. As the train pulled into the station, he felt a sense of relief. The crisp morning air and the bustling energy of the platform were a welcome change from the silent, accusing presence of Dolly.

He found Anna in her carriage, looking as elegant and composed as ever. There was a subtle grace in her movements, a quiet strength in her eyes that always commanded respect. As they embraced, Stepan felt a rush of genuine affection. "Stiva!" she exclaimed, her voice warm and melodic. "It is so good to see you."
"And you, Anna," he replied, beaming. "You look more radiant than ever. How was the journey?"
"Long, but uneventful," she said, settling back into the plush cushions. "But tell me, how is Dolly? And the children?"
Stepan's face clouded for a moment. "Dolly is... well, she is Dolly. The children are growing fast. But we have much to discuss, Anna. Things are not as they should be at home."

Anna listened intently as they drove through the streets of Moscow. She did not interrupt, but her occasional nods and sympathetic glances told him that she understood more than he was saying. She had always had a gift for empathy, a way of making people feel heard and understood without the need for many words.

By the time they reached the house, Stepan felt a weight had been lifted from his shoulders. He knew that if anyone could bridge the gap between him and Dolly, it was Anna. Her presence alone seemed to bring a sense of order and calm to the chaotic household.

The meeting between the two women was at first strained. Dolly, her face pale and etched with the fatigue of many sleepless nights, received Anna with a formal, almost chilly politeness. But Anna, with her characteristic tact and warmth, slowly began to thaw the ice. She spoke of the children, of common friends, of trivialities that served to ease the tension.
"Dolly, my dear," Anna said softly, taking her sister-in-law's hand, "I know how much you are suffering. Stiva has told me everything."
Dolly's eyes filled with tears. "He has? Then he has told you of his betrayal? Of his utter lack of respect for me and our family?"
"He is a man, Dolly," Anna replied gently. "And men, unfortunately, are prone to such weaknesses. But he loves you, in his own way. And he is truly sorry for the pain he has caused you."
"Is he?" Dolly asked bitterly. "Or is he only sorry that he was caught?"

Anna did not answer immediately. She knew that there was truth in Dolly's words, but she also knew that a reconciliation was necessary for the sake of the children and the stability of the family. "We must all make sacrifices, Dolly," she said at last. "For the sake of those we love."

Over the next few days, Anna worked tirelessly to bring the couple back together. She spent hours talking to Dolly, listening to her grievances, and offering words of comfort and advice. She also spoke to Stepan, urging him to be more attentive and to show Dolly the respect and affection she deserved.

Slowly, almost imperceptibly, the atmosphere in the house began to change. The children, sensing the easing of tension, became more cheerful and less disruptive. The servants, too, seemed to take their cue from Anna, and the household began to run more smoothly.

But while Anna was occupied with her brother's problems, she was also grappling with her own. Her marriage to Karenin, though stable and respected, lacked the passion and emotional depth she craved. She felt a sense of restlessness, a yearning for something more than the formal, structured life she led in Petersburg.

It was during this time that she first encountered Count Vronsky. He was a frequent visitor to the house, a friend of Stepan's and a rising star in Moscow society. From their first meeting, there was an unmistakable spark between them. Vronsky was drawn to Anna's beauty and intelligence, while Anna was intrigued by his charm and the sense of excitement he brought with him.

They met at balls, at dinner parties, and at the theater. Each encounter only served to deepen their mutual attraction. They spoke of literature, of art, of their shared disdain for the superficiality of society. For Anna, Vronsky represented a world of passion and freedom that she had only ever dreamed of.

But she was also acutely aware of the dangers of such an attraction. She was a married woman, a mother, and a member of one of the most prominent families in Russia. An affair would not only ruin her reputation but also bring shame upon her husband and son. She tried to distance herself from Vronsky, to focus on her duties and her family. But the more she tried to resist, the more powerful the attraction became.

Vronsky, for his part, was relentless. He pursued her with a quiet, persistent intensity that both frightened and exhilarated her. He made no secret of his feelings, and his every look and gesture seemed to speak of his devotion.

The climax of their burgeoning relationship came at a grand ball given by the Governor. Anna, dressed in a stunning black velvet gown, was the center of attention. As she moved through the room, she felt Vronsky's eyes upon her. When he finally asked her to dance, she felt a surge of both dread and anticipation.

As they swirled across the floor, the rest of the world seemed to fade away. There was only the music, the warmth of his hand, and the intensity of his gaze. In that moment, Anna knew that she was lost. She knew that she could no longer deny her feelings for Vronsky, and that her life would never be the same again.

After the dance, they found a quiet corner in the conservatory. The air was thick with the scent of exotic flowers, and the distant sound of the orchestra provided a romantic backdrop. Vronsky took her hand and looked into her eyes.
"Anna," he whispered, his voice full of emotion, "I cannot live without you. You are everything to me."

Anna felt a wave of both joy and despair wash over her. She knew that she should walk away, that she should end it there and then. But she couldn't. She looked at him, her eyes full of tears, and knew that her fate was sealed.

The ball was at its height when Anna and Vronsky stood in the conservatory. Anna was breathing quickly, and her eyes, which usually had a look of calm dignity, now sparkled with a frightened but intense light. She felt as though she had crossed a boundary from which there was no returning. Vronsky, too, was changed. His usual cold, composed manner had vanished, replaced by an eager, almost desperate passion.

"I cannot go on like this," Vronsky said, his voice dropping to a low, forceful whisper. "You must know what you have done to me. From the moment I saw you at the station, my life has been yours. I don't care about the world, or my career, or anything else. I only care about you."

Anna looked at him, and for a moment, the image of her husband, Alexey Alexandrovitch, and her son, Seryozha, flashed before her mind. She felt a pang of guilt so sharp it was like a physical blow. But then she looked back at Vronsky, at his handsome, earnest face, and the guilt was swept away by a wave of longing.
"You mustn't say such things," she whispered, though her hand remained in his. "You know it is impossible. I am not free."

"Impossible?" Vronsky repeated, a bitter smile touching his lips. "Nothing is impossible for those who love as I love you. You are unhappy, Anna. I see it in your eyes. You are living a life that is a lie. Why continue it?"
Anna shook her head, tears finally spilling over. "It is not that simple. There is Seryozha... there is my duty."

"Duty is a word used by those who are afraid to live," Vronsky countered. He stepped closer, so close she could feel the heat radiating from him. "Don't let fear decide your life. Tell me you don't feel what I feel. Tell me you don't love me, and I will leave you forever."

Anna opened her mouth to speak the lie, but the words died in her throat. She looked into his eyes and saw her own reflection-a woman who was finally awake, finally feeling the terrifying, beautiful weight of true emotion. Instead of a denial, a sob escaped her, and she leaned her forehead against his chest.

Vronsky threw his arms around her, holding her with a fierce, protective strength. He knew he had won, but he also knew the battle had only just begun. They stood there for a long time, two people adrift in a sea of societal expectations, clinging to each other as if to life itself.
When Anna finally pulled away, she was pale but resolute. "I must go," she said, her voice steady now. "I must return to Petersburg tomorrow."
"I will follow you," Vronsky said instantly. "I will be where you are."

Anna didn't protest. She knew it was useless. As she walked back into the ballroom, she felt as if she were moving through a dream. The lights, the music, the laughter of the guests-all of it seemed distant and meaningless. She found her brother, Stepan Arkadyevitch, and told him she was tired and wished to leave. Stiva, seeing the transformed expression on her face, didn't question her. He escorted her to her carriage, his own heart heavy with a premonition of the storm that was about to break.

As the carriage rolled through the dark streets of Moscow, Anna closed her eyes. She thought of the cold, orderly house in Petersburg, of her husband's rhythmic, bloodless voice, and of the suffocating peace she had once called happiness. That life was over. Ahead of her lay a path filled with uncertainty and scandal, but for the first time in years, she felt truly alive.

But at that very moment the princess came in. There was a look of horror on her face when she saw them alone, and their disturbed faces. Levin bowed to her, and said nothing. Kitty did not speak nor lift her eyes. "Thank God, she has refused him," thought the mother, and her face lighted up with the habitual smile with which she greeted her guests on Thursdays. She sat down and began questioning Levin about his life in the country. He sat down again, waiting for other visitors to arrive, in order to retreat unnoticed.

Five minutes later there came in a friend of Kitty's, married the preceding winter, Countess Nordston. She was a thin, sallow, sickly and nervous woman, with brilliant black eyes. She was fond of Kitty, and her affection for her showed itself, as the affection of married women for girls always does, in a desire to get Kitty married after her own ideal of happiness; she wanted her to marry Vronsky. Levin she had often met at the Shtcherbatskys' in the early part of the winter, and she had always disliked him. her favorite weapon against him was irony, and she began now to use it.

"I am so glad you have come, Konstantin Dmitrievitch," she said, with the same smile as ever. "Why is it you have such a contempt for our poor Moscow?.... What has become of the cattle and the district councils?"

Levin felt that he was being turned into ridicule, and he wanted to say something in self-defense; but he could not find the right words. He felt that in the presence of this woman, he was indeed the "savage" Oblonsky had called him.
"I have no contempt for Moscow," he answered, trying to speak calmly. "I only prefer the country."
"And the cattle? And the district councils?" the Countess persisted. "You must tell us all about them. We are so interested."

Kitty saw that Levin was being made uncomfortable, and she felt sorry for him. She knew that he was a man of great intellect and character, and she did not like to see him being turned into ridicule. She tried to change the conversation, but the Countess would not let her.
"And how is your brother, Sergey Ivanovitch?" the Countess asked. "I hear he has written a new book. Is it true?"

"Yes, it is true," answered Levin, and he felt a sense of relief at the change of subject. He began to talk about his brother's book, and for a few minutes the conversation was more pleasant. But then the Countess returned to her irony.
"And what do you think of the new phase of the district councils?" she asked. "I hear they are going to do great things."

Levin felt his anger rising. He knew that she was not really interested in the district councils, and that she was only trying to make him look foolish. He was about to give a sharp answer when he saw Kitty looking at him with a look of entreaty. He mastered himself and gave a quiet, sensible answer.

The arrival of more guests put an end to the conversation. Levin found himself in a corner of the room, watching the people. He saw Vronsky arrive, and he saw the way Kitty's face lighted up when she saw him. He felt a pang of jealousy and despair. He knew that Vronsky was the man she loved, and that he had no chance against him.

Vronsky was as usual charming and attentive. He talked to Kitty and her mother, and he was the center of attention. Levin felt like an outsider, a "savage" who had no place in this refined and elegant world. He decided to leave, and he was about to say good-bye when Stepan Arkadyevitch came up to him.
"Well, how are you getting on?" Oblonsky asked, with his usual bright smile. "Have you spoken to her?"
"Yes, I have," answered Levin, "and it's all over."
"What! She refused you?"
"Yes."
Oblonsky's face became grave for a moment. "I'm sorry, my dear boy. But don't take it too much to heart. There are other girls in the world."

"Not for me," said Levin, and he went out of the room. He felt a sense of utter loneliness and misery. He walked through the streets of Moscow, not knowing where he was going. He felt that his life was over, and that he had nothing to live for.

He went back to his brother's house, and spent the night in a state of deep depression. He thought of Kitty, and he saw her face before him, with that look of horror and pity. He felt that he had been a fool to think that she could ever love him, and he wished that he had never come to Moscow.

In the morning he decided to go back to the country. He felt that he could not stay in Moscow any longer. He packed his things and went to the station. As the train pulled out of the city, he felt a sense of relief. He was going back to his own world, where he was respected and understood, and where he could forget about Kitty and Vronsky.

But as he traveled back to the country, he found that he could not forget. Kitty's face was always before him, and her words were always in his ears. He felt a sense of longing and sadness that he could not shake off. He realized that his love for Kitty was not something that could be forgotten, and that it would stay with him for the rest of his life.

When he arrived at his estate, he was greeted by the servants and the villagers. They were all glad to see him, and they felt that his presence brought a sense of order and peace to the place. Levin threw himself into his work, and he spent his days in the fields and the barns. He tried to drown his grief in hard work, and for a while it seemed to work. He felt a sense of satisfaction in his achievements, and he began to feel more like his old self.

But in the evenings, when he was alone in his house, the old feelings would come back. He would think of Kitty, and he would wonder what she was doing. He would wonder if she was happy with Vronsky, and if she ever thought of him. He felt a sense of emptiness and loneliness that no amount of work could fill.

One evening, as he was sitting in his study, he received a letter from Stepan Arkadyevitch. Oblonsky wrote that Kitty was not happy, and that her health was failing. He said that the engagement with Vronsky had been broken off, and that Vronsky had left for Petersburg. He urged Levin to come back to Moscow and try once more.

Levin felt a surge of hope and joy. He decided to go back to Moscow at once. He felt that this was his last chance, and he was determined to take it. He arrived in Moscow the next day, and he went straight to the Shtcherbatskys' house.

He found Kitty in the garden. She was sitting on a bench, and she looked pale and thin. When she saw Levin, her face lighted up with a look of joy and relief. She got up and went towards him, and they both stood there for a moment, not saying a word.
"I'm so glad you came," she said at last, and her voice was full of emotion.
"I had to come," answered Levin. "I couldn't stay away."

They sat down on the bench, and they began to talk. Levin told her how much he loved her, and how he had suffered since he had left her. Kitty told him how she had realized that it was him she loved, and how she had been miserable without him. They both felt a sense of rapture and bliss, and they knew that they were destined to be together.

Levin spoke to the Prince and the Princess, and they both gave their consent to the marriage. The wedding was fixed for the following month, and preparations began at once. This time, there was no doubt or hesitation. They both knew that they were doing the right thing, and they were both very happy.

The wedding was a grand affair, and everyone in Moscow society was there. Kitty was a beautiful bride, and Levin was a proud and happy groom. They both felt a sense of awe and reverence as they stood at the altar. They knew that they were entering into a sacred bond, and that they would love and cherish each other for the rest of their lives.

After the ceremony, there was a reception at the Shtcherbatskys' house. There was music, dancing, and a magnificent feast. Everyone was in high spirits, and the atmosphere was one of joy and celebration. Levin and Kitty were the center of attention, and they both looked radiant and happy.

In the evening, they left for the country. They were going to live at Levin's estate, and they were both looking forward to their new life together. As they drove away from Moscow, they felt a sense of gratitude and peace. They knew that they had many challenges ahead of them, but they also knew that they had each other, and that their love would sustain them.

When they arrived at the estate, they were greeted by the servants and the villagers. Everyone was glad to see them, and they felt a sense of home and belonging. Levin showed Kitty around the house and the farm, and she was delighted with everything she saw. She felt that this was the place where she belonged, and that she would be happy here with Levin.

The first few months of their marriage were a time of adjustment and discovery. They learned to live together and to understand each other's needs and desires. They had their share of disagreements and misunderstandings, but they always managed to resolve them through love and patience. They were both very busy with the work on the farm and the house, and they felt a sense of satisfaction and achievement.

As time went on, their love grew deeper and stronger. They became true partners in life, sharing their joys and sorrows, and supporting each other through everything. They had children, and they raised them with love and care. They lived a simple and honest life, and they were respected and loved by everyone who knew them.

Levin felt that he had found the true meaning of life. He realized that happiness does not come from wealth or status, but from love, family, and a sense of purpose. He felt a sense of gratitude for everything he had, and he knew that he was a truly fortunate man. He looked at Kitty, and he saw in her eyes the reflection of his own love and happiness. He knew that they would be together forever, and that their love would never end.

At the end of the evening Kitty told her mother of her conversation with Levin, and in spite of all the pity she felt for Levin, she was glad at the thought that she had received an offer. She had no doubt that she had acted rightly. But after she had gone to bed, for a long while she could not sleep. One impression pursued her relentlessly. It was Levin's face, with his scowling brows, and his kind eyes looking out in dark dejection below them, as he stood listening to her father, and glancing at her and at Vronsky. And she felt so sorry for him that tears came into her eyes. But immediately she thought of the man for whom she had given him up. She vividly recalled his manly, resolute face, his noble self-possession, and the good nature conspicuous in everything towards everyone. She remembered

the love of him she loved, and she felt joyful again, and lay down on the pillow with a smile of happiness. "I am sorry, I am sorry, but what could I do? It's not my fault," she said to herself; but an inner voice said something else. Whether she regretted having puffed up Levin's hopes, or having rejected him, she did not know. But her happiness was poisoned by doubts. "Lord, have mercy on us; Lord, have mercy on us; Lord, have mercy on us!" she said to herself, till she fell asleep.
Meanwhile there was taking place downstairs in the prince's little study one of those scenes so often repeated between the parents on account of their favorite daughter.

"What? This is what!" shouted the prince, waving his arms, and at once wrapping his squirrel-lined dressing-gown round him again. "You've no pride, no dignity; you're disgracing, ruining your daughter by this vulgar, stupid matchmaking!"
"But for mercy's sake, prince, what have I done?" said the princess, almost crying.

She, the princess, happy and satisfied after her daughter's conversation with Levin, had gone to the prince to say goodnight as usual, and though she had meant to say nothing of Levin's offer and Kitty's refusal, she had let it out to the prince that the matter was as good as settled with Vronsky, and that he would speak as soon as his mother arrived. And at those words the prince had all at once flown into a passion, and begun to use unseemly language.

"What have you done? I'll tell you what. In the first place, you're hunting a young man who is the talk of all Moscow, and that's a disgrace. If you want to marry your daughter, marry her to a man who's an equal, and not to a popinjay who's only thinking of his own pleasure. Vronsky! He's a piece of trash. Everyone knows what he is. He'll play with her and throw her away, as he has done with dozens of others. And Levin-Levin is a man, a real man, worth a thousand of your Vronskys!"
"But Levin is a savage! He has no manners, no position..."
"Position! What do I care for his position? He's an honest man, and he loves her. And Kitty loved him too, until you put this Vronsky into her head. You're making her miserable, I tell you!"

The princess began to cry in earnest now. "How can you say such things? I only want my daughter's happiness. Vronsky is a brilliant match. His mother is coming to Moscow expressly to arrange the matter."

"His mother! Another old fool," muttered the prince. "I tell you, I won't have it. If Vronsky wants her, let him come and ask me like a man. But I won't have my daughter being chased after like a common girl."

The prince turned his back on his wife and began to pace the room. He was deeply troubled. He liked Levin, and he had seen the look of pain on his face when he left. He knew that Kitty was making a mistake, but he also knew that his wife would not listen to reason.

The princess, meanwhile, was thinking of the future. She saw Kitty as the wife of one of the most brilliant men in Petersburg society. She saw her at court, at the center of everything. She could not understand why the prince was being so difficult.
"You'll see," she said, wiping her eyes. "Vronsky will speak, and Kitty will be happy. And you'll be the first to admit you were wrong."
"We shall see," said the prince. "But I doubt it."

He went to bed, but he could not sleep. He thought of his daughters, of Dolly and her unhappy marriage, and he feared for Kitty. He knew that the world was a cruel place, and that a young girl's heart was easily broken.

In the morning, the atmosphere in the house was tense. Kitty, sensing the discord between her parents, was quiet and withdrawn. She spent the morning in her room, thinking of Levin and Vronsky. She felt a sense of confusion and uncertainty that she had never felt before.

At noon, a carriage drove up to the door. It was the Countess Vronsky, come to call on the princess. The princess was delighted, and she hurried to receive her guest. The prince, however, remained in his study, refusing to come out.

The two women talked for a long time. The countess was a woman of the world, sophisticated and charming. She spoke of her son's devotion to Kitty, and of her own desire to see them married. The princess was enchanted, and she felt that all her dreams for Kitty were about to come true.

But as the day went on, Kitty's doubts grew. She saw the way her father looked at her, and she felt the weight of his disapproval. She remembered Levin's face, and she felt a sense of loss that she could not explain. She began to realize that her decision was not as simple as she had thought, and that her happiness was not as certain as she had hoped.
Vronsky followed the guard to the carriage, and at the door of the compartment he stopped short to make room for a lady who was getting out.

With the insight of a man of the world, from one glance at this lady's appearance Vronsky classified her as belonging to the best society. He begged pardon, and was getting into the carriage, but felt he must glance at her once more; not that she was very beautiful, not on account of the elegance and modest grace which were apparent in her whole figure, but because in the expression of her charming face, as she passed close by him, there was something peculiarly caressing and soft. As he looked round, she too turned her head. Her shining gray eyes, that looked dark from the thick lashes, rested with friendly,

attentive inquiry on his face, as though she were recognizing him, and then at once passed to the passing crowd, as though seeking some one. In that brief look Vronsky had time to notice the suppressed eagerness which played over her face, and flitted between her sparkling eyes and the faint smile that curved her rosy lips. It was as though her whole nature were so brimming over with something that, against her will, it showed itself now in the flash of her eyes, and now in her smile. She deliberately shrouded the light in her eyes, but it shone against her will in the faintly perceptible smile.

Stepan Arkadyevitch was standing at the door of the carriage, and on seeing her he gave a start of joy. Vronsky's eyes followed her, and he saw that it was the sister of Stepan Arkadyevitch, Anna Arkadyevna Karenina.
"Ah, Stiva! you've come!" she said, and her voice was as charming as her face. She kissed him, and then, after a brief exchange of words, she turned her head and looked at Vronsky once more.
"Vronsky, let me introduce you to my sister," said Stepan Arkadyevitch.

Vronsky bowed, and she responded with a slight inclination of her head. He felt that she had been told about him, and that she was interested in him. He felt a sense of excitement and pleasure, and he knew that he wanted to see her again.
"I am very glad to meet you," she said, and her voice was full of warmth and sincerity.
"And I am very glad to meet you," replied Vronsky.

They talked for a few minutes, and then Stepan Arkadyevitch led her away to their carriage. Vronsky watched them go, and he felt a sense of regret. He wished that he could have stayed and talked to her longer. He felt that she was a woman of great intelligence and charm, and he was intrigued by her.

He went back to his own carriage and sat down. He was thinking of Anna, and he could not get her out of his mind. He saw her face, her eyes, her smile, and he heard her voice. He felt a sense of anticipation and excitement that he had never felt before. He knew that he would see her again, and he was looking forward to it.
"Well, what then? Oh, nothing. It's good for me, and good for her." And he began wondering where to finish the evening.

The engine had already whistled in the distance. A few moments later the platform quivered, and with puffs of steam, driven downwards by the frost, the engine rolled in, with the connecting rod of the middle wheel slowly and rhythmically moving, and the muffled, smothered figure of the engine driver covered with hoarfrost. Behind the tender, more and more slowly, and shaking the platform, the luggage-van passed with a dog whining inside; at last the passenger carriages arrived, jarring as they came to a standstill.

A smart guardsman, jumping out while the train was still in motion, whistled, and after him there began to get out one by one the impatient passengers: an officer of the guards, holding himself erect and looking severely about him; a nimble little merchant with a satchel, smiling gaily; a peasant with a sack over his shoulder.

Vronsky, standing beside Oblonsky, watched the carriages and the passengers, totally forgetting his mother. What he had just heard about Madame Karenina excited and interested him. He wanted to meet her. He had an instinctive feeling that he was going to meet some one quite exceptional.
"Countess Vronskaya is in that carriage," said the smart guardsman, going up to Vronsky.
Vronsky followed him. At the door of the carriage he stopped to make room for a lady who was getting out.

And with the insight of a man of the world, from one glance at this lady's appearance Vronsky classified her as belonging to the best society. He begged pardon, and was getting into the carriage, but felt he must glance at her once more; not that she was very beautiful, not on account of the elegance and modest grace which were apparent in her whole figure, but because in the expression of her charming face, as she passed close by him, there was something peculiarly caressing and soft.

As he looked round, she too turned her head. Her shining gray eyes, that looked dark from the thick lashes, rested with friendly, attentive inquiry on his face, as though she were recognizing him, and then at once passed to the passing crowd, as though seeking some one. In that brief look Vronsky had time to notice the suppressed eagerness which played over her face, and flitted between her sparkling eyes and the faint smile that curved her rosy lips. It was as though her whole nature were so brimming over with something that, against her will, it showed itself now in the flash of her eyes, and now in her smile. She deliberately shrouded the light in her eyes, but it shone against her will in the faintly perceptible smile.

Stepan Arkadyevitch was standing at the door of the carriage, and on seeing her he gave a start of joy. Vronsky's eyes followed her, and he saw that it was the sister of Stepan Arkadyevitch, Anna Arkadyevna Karenina.
"Ah, Stiva! you've come!" she said, and her voice was as charming as her face. She kissed him, and then, after a brief exchange of words, she turned her head and looked at Vronsky once more.
"Vronsky, let me introduce you to my sister," said Stepan Arkadyevitch.

Vronsky bowed, and she responded with a slight inclination of her head. He felt that she had been told about him, and that she was interested in him. He felt a sense of excitement and pleasure, and he knew that he wanted to see her again.
"I am very glad to meet you," she said, and her voice was full of warmth and sincerity.
"And I am very glad to meet you," replied Vronsky.

They talked for a few minutes, and then Stepan Arkadyevitch led her away to their carriage. Vronsky watched them go, and he felt a sense of regret. He wished that he could have stayed and talked to her longer. He felt that she was a woman of great intelligence and charm, and he was intrigued by her.

He went back to his own mother's carriage. The old Countess, a withered woman with black eyes and little ringlets, was already out on the platform. She was pleased to see her son and began talking to him at once about her journey and the lady who had been her companion.

"If you only knew what a charming woman I've been traveling with!" she said. "Madame Karenina. Her brother met her; do you know him? He's in the government here. She told me all about her son; she has such a delightful way of talking."
"I have just been introduced to her," Vronsky replied, still under the impression of that brief meeting.
But their conversation was interrupted. A sudden commotion arose on the platform. People were running toward the end of the train. Vronsky and his mother looked toward the crowd.
"What is it? What's happened?" asked the Countess.
A railway guard, looking pale and terrified, ran past them. "A man's been crushed! Under the wheels!" he shouted.
The horror of the accident cast a shadow over the arrival. Vronsky went to see if he could be of any help, while his mother waited, trembling. When he returned, his face was grave.
"He is dead," Vronsky said shortly. "A watchman, it seems. He didn't hear the train."

Anna Arkadyevna, who had returned to the spot with her brother, was visibly shaken. Her face was pale, and her eyes were wide with distress. "A bad omen," she whispered to Stepan Arkadyevitch, her voice trembling.
"Nonsense, Anna," Oblonsky said, trying to comfort her, though he too looked disturbed. "It's a terrible accident, but it has nothing to do with you."
Vronsky stepped forward. "Can I be of any service, Madame? My carriage is at your disposal if your brother's is not ready."
"Thank you, Count," she replied, regaining some of her composure. "My brother's carriage is here. But thank you for your kindness."

She bowed to him and to the old Countess, and walked away with her brother. Vronsky stood looking after her. The tragic event seemed to have linked them in some strange, inexplicable way. He felt that this day, which had begun so normally, was the start of something momentous in his life.

He saw her into the carriage, and as it drove away, Anna looked out of the window. Their eyes met for a fleeting second, and in her look Vronsky saw a mixture of sadness and that same suppressed light he had noticed before.
"Come, Alexey," his mother said, pulling at his arm. "Let us get away from this dreadful place."

Vronsky followed his mother to their carriage, but his thoughts were far away. He was thinking of the woman with the gray eyes, and the dark shadow of the death on the rails that seemed to follow her.
"Then you're quite satisfied?" said Oblonsky. "I am so glad for you. But tell me, what did you think of the sister?"
"Of whom?"
"Of Anna Arkadyevna."
"I thought her very charming. But here is the train."

The engine had already whistled in the distance. A few moments later the platform quivered, and with puffs of steam, driven downwards by the frost, the engine rolled in, with the connecting rod of the middle wheel slowly and rhythmically moving, and the muffled, smothered figure of the engine driver covered with hoarfrost. Behind the tender, more and more slowly, and shaking the platform, the luggage-van passed with a dog whining inside; at last the passenger carriages arrived, jarring as they came to a standstill.

A smart guardsman, jumping out while the train was still in motion, whistled, and after him there began to get out one by one the impatient passengers: an officer of the guards, holding himself erect and looking severely about him; a nimble little merchant with a satchel, smiling gaily; a peasant with a sack over his shoulder.

Vronsky, standing beside Oblonsky, watched the carriages and the passengers, totally forgetting his mother. What he had just heard about Madame Karenina excited and interested him. He wanted to see her again. He had an instinctive feeling that she was the woman he had seen getting out of the carriage at the previous station.
"Countess Vronskaya is in that carriage," said the smart guardsman, going up to Vronsky.
Vronsky followed him. At the door of the carriage he stopped to make room for a lady who was getting out.

And with the insight of a man of the world, from one glance at this lady's appearance Vronsky classified her as belonging to the best society. He begged pardon, and was getting into the carriage, but felt he must glance at her once more; not that she was very beautiful, not on account of the elegance and modest grace which were apparent in her whole figure, but because in the expression of her charming face, as she passed close by him, there was something peculiarly caressing and soft.

As he looked round, she too turned her head. Her shining gray eyes, that looked dark from the thick lashes, rested with friendly, attentive inquiry on his face, as though she were recognizing him, and then at once passed to the passing crowd, as though seeking some one. In that brief look Vronsky had time to notice the suppressed eagerness which played over her face, and flitted between her sparkling eyes and the faint smile that curved her rosy lips. It was as though her whole nature were so brimming over with something that, against her will, it showed itself now in the flash of her eyes, and now in her smile. She deliberately shrouded the light in her eyes, but it shone against her will in the faintly perceptible smile.

Stepan Arkadyevitch was standing at the door of the carriage, and on seeing her he gave a start of joy. Vronsky's eyes followed her, and he saw that it was the sister of Stepan Arkadyevitch, Anna Arkadyevna Karenina.
"Ah, Stiva! you've come!" she said, and her voice was as charming as her face. She kissed him, and then, after a brief exchange of words, she turned her head and looked at Vronsky once more.
"Vronsky, let me introduce you to my sister," said Stepan Arkadyevitch.

Vronsky bowed, and she responded with a slight inclination of her head. He felt that she had been told about him, and that she was interested in him. He felt a sense of excitement and pleasure, and he knew that he wanted to see her again.
"I am very glad to meet you," she said, and her voice was full of warmth and sincerity.
"And I am very glad to meet you," replied Vronsky.

They talked for a few minutes, and then Stepan Arkadyevitch led her away to their carriage. Vronsky watched them go, and he felt a sense of regret. He wished that he could have stayed and talked to her longer. He felt that she was a woman of great intelligence and charm, and he was intrigued by her.

He went back to his own carriage and sat down. He was thinking of Anna, and he could not get her out of his mind. He saw her face, her eyes, her smile, and he heard her voice. He felt a sense of anticipation and excitement that he had never felt before. He knew that he would see her again, and he was looking forward to it.

The train arrived in Moscow, and Vronsky went to his hotel. He was tired, but he was also happy. He had met Anna, and he felt that his life had been changed. He knew that he was in love, and he was ready for whatever lay ahead.

Next day at eleven o'clock in the morning Vronsky drove to the station of the Petersburg railway to meet his mother, and the first person he came across on the great flight of steps was Oblonsky, who was expecting his sister by the same train.
"Ah! your excellency!" cried Oblonsky, "whom are you meeting?"
"My mother," Vronsky responded, smiling, as everyone did who met Oblonsky. He shook hands with him, and together they ascended the steps. "She is to be here from Petersburg today."
"I was looking out for you till two o'clock last night. Where did you go after the Shtcherbatskys'?"
"Home," answered Vronsky. "I must own I felt so well content yesterday after the Shtcherbatskys' that I didn't care to go anywhere else."
"I know those well-contented moments!" said Stepan Arkadyevitch. "I am so glad for you. But tell me, what did you think of the sister?"
"Of whom?"
"Of Anna Arkadyevna."
"I thought her very charming. But here is the train."

The engine had already whistled in the distance. A few moments later the platform quivered, and with puffs of steam, driven downwards by the frost, the engine rolled in, with the connecting rod of the middle wheel slowly and rhythmically moving, and the muffled, smothered figure of the engine driver covered with hoarfrost. Behind the tender, more and more slowly, and shaking the platform, the luggage-van passed with a dog whining inside; at last the passenger carriages arrived, jarring as they came to a standstill.

A smart guardsman, jumping out while the train was still in motion, whistled, and after him there began to get out one by one the impatient passengers: an officer of the guards, holding himself erect and looking severely about him; a nimble little merchant with a satchel, smiling gaily; a peasant with a sack over his shoulder.

Vronsky, standing beside Oblonsky, watched the carriages and the passengers, totally forgetting his mother. What he had just heard about Madame Karenina excited and interested him. He wanted to meet her. He had an instinctive feeling that he was going to meet some one quite exceptional.

When Anna went into the room, Dolly was sitting in the little drawing-room with a white-headed fat little boy, already like his father, giving him a lesson in French reading. As the boy read, he kept twisting and trying to tear off a button that was nearly off his jacket. His mother had several times taken his hand from it, but the fat little hand went back to the button again. His mother pulled the button off and put it in her pocket.

"Keep your hands still, Grisha," she said, and she took up her work, a coverlet she had long been making. She always set to work on it at depressed moments, and now she knitted at it nervously, twitching her fingers and counting the stitches. Though she had sent word the day before to her husband that it was nothing to her whether his sister came or not, she had everything ready for her sister-in-law's arrival, and was looking forward to seeing her.

Anna, in her traveling dress, came into the room. Her face was pale, but her eyes were full of a light that seemed to be concentrated in them. When she saw Dolly, she stopped for a moment, and then went quickly towards her. Dolly, too, got up and went to meet her. They kissed each other, and for a few moments neither of them spoke.
"I am so glad you have come, Anna," said Dolly at last, and her voice was full of emotion.
"And I am so glad to see you, Dolly," replied Anna. "How are you? And the children?"

They sat down and began to talk. Anna told Dolly about her journey and about the people she had met. She spoke of Vronsky, and of the impression he had made on her. Dolly listened with interest, and she could see that Anna was deeply moved.
"He is a very remarkable man, Dolly," said Anna. "There is something about him... something that I have never seen in anyone else."
Dolly smiled. "I know him, Anna. He is one of the most brilliant men in Petersburg society. But be careful. He is a man who can be very dangerous."
Anna laughed. "Dangerous? In what way?"

"In the way that men like him are always dangerous," replied Dolly. "He is a man who knows how to win a woman's heart, and then... but we won't talk about that now. Tell me more about your life in Petersburg."

Anna began to talk about her husband and her son. She spoke of the cold and formal life they led, and of the sense of emptiness she often felt. Dolly listened with sympathy, and she could see that Anna was not happy. She felt a sense of pity for her, and she wanted to help her.
"You must stay with us for a while, Anna," said Dolly. "It will do you good to get away from Petersburg. We will have a quiet time, and you can rest."
"I should like that very much, Dolly," replied Anna. "I need a rest. I feel so tired... so tired of everything."

They spent the rest of the morning talking and playing with the children. Anna was very fond of children, and she soon won the hearts of Dolly's little ones. They laughed and played together, and for a while Anna forgot about her troubles.

In the afternoon, Stepan Arkadyevitch came home. He was delighted to see his sister, and he greeted her with his usual warmth and affection. He told her about the latest news from Moscow, and about the parties and dinners that were being planned.
"You must come to the ball at the Governor's house tomorrow, Anna," he said. "Everyone will be there, and it will be a grand affair."
Anna hesitated. "I don't know, Stiva. I'm not sure I'm in the mood for a ball."
"Nonsense!" said Stepan Arkadyevitch. "It will do you good. You need to get out and enjoy yourself. And besides, Vronsky will be there."

At the mention of Vronsky's name, Anna felt a surge of excitement. She knew that she wanted to see him again, and she knew that the ball would be the perfect opportunity. She decided to go, and she began to look forward to it.

The next day was spent in preparation for the ball. Anna and Dolly went shopping, and they bought new dresses and jewelry. They spent hours in front of the mirror, trying on different things and deciding what to wear. They were both very excited, and the atmosphere in the house was one of joy and anticipation.

In the evening, they drove to the Governor's house. The ball was in full swing when they arrived. The rooms were filled with people in elegant dresses and uniforms. The music was playing, and the air was thick with the scent of flowers and perfume.

Anna looked for Vronsky, and at last she saw him. He was standing with a group of friends, and he looked more handsome than ever. When he saw Anna, his face lighted up with a look of joy and recognition. He went up to her and spoke to her, and they both felt a sense of rapture and bliss.
"I'm so glad you came, Anna," he said, and his voice was full of emotion.
"I had to come," replied Anna. "I wanted to see you."

They danced together, and they spent the rest of the evening talking and laughing. They were both so happy that they did not notice the people around them. They felt that they were the only two people in the world, and that nothing else mattered.

But even then, Anna felt a sense of guilt. She knew that she was a married woman, and that she was doing something wrong. She knew that her husband would not approve, and that she was risking her reputation. But she could not help herself. She was in love with Vronsky, and she was ready to face anything for his sake.
When the ball was over, Vronsky led Anna to her carriage. He took her hand and kissed it, and he looked into her eyes with a look of love and devotion.
"I will see you tomorrow," he said.
"Yes," replied Anna. "Tomorrow."

As the carriage drove away, Anna looked back at Vronsky. She felt a sense of happiness and peace that she had never known. She knew that her life had been changed, and she was ready for the challenge.

The next few weeks were a time of happiness and discovery for Anna. She spent every day with Vronsky, and they became closer and closer. They went for long walks in the park, they went to the theater and the opera, and they spent hours talking about their love and their future.

They were both very happy, but they were also living in fear. They knew that their relationship was a secret and a scandal, and that if it were discovered, it would mean ruin for both of them. They had to be careful, they had to be discreet.

Anna's husband, Alexey Alexandrovitch, began to notice a change in her behavior. He saw that she was more cheerful and more energetic, and he wondered why. He began to ask her questions, but Anna was careful not to give anything away. She told him that she was enjoying her stay in Moscow, and that she was feeling much better.

But Alexey Alexandrovitch was not satisfied. He began to watch Anna more closely, and he began to suspect that something was wrong. He noticed the way she looked when Vronsky's name was mentioned, and he saw the way she went out every day.

One day, he followed her when she went out to meet Vronsky. He saw them together in the park, and he saw the way they looked at each other. He felt a sense of horror and despair. He knew that his wife was having an affair, and he did not know what to do.

He went home and spent the night in a state of deep depression. He thought of his marriage and his son, and he felt a sense of betrayal. He realized that his life was over, and that he had nothing to live for.

The next morning, he confronted Anna. He told her what he had seen, and he asked her to explain. Anna was terrified, but she knew that she could no longer hide the truth. She broke down and confessed everything. She told him that she loved Vronsky, and that she wanted to be with him.

Alexey Alexandrovitch was devastated. He told Anna that he would not grant her a divorce, and that she would have to live with the consequences of her actions. He told her that she would have to leave his house, and that she would never see her son again.

Anna was heartbroken. She loved her son more than anything in the world, and the thought of never seeing him again was more than she could bear. She pleaded with her husband, but he was firm. He told her that he had made his decision, and that nothing would change it.

Anna left the house in a state of despair. She went to Vronsky and told him what had happened. Vronsky was also devastated, but he promised to stand by her. He told her that he would take care of her, and that they would build a new life together.

They left Moscow and went to Italy. They lived in a beautiful villa on the shores of Lake Como, and for a while they were happy. They spent their days walking and sailing, and their nights talking and loving. They felt that they were the only two people in the world, and that nothing else mattered.

But as time went on, their happiness began to fade. Anna could not forget her son, and she felt a sense of guilt and longing that she could not shake off. She began to miss her old life in Petersburg, and she felt a sense of loneliness and isolation.

Vronsky, too, was beginning to feel the strain. He was a man of action and ambition, and he found the quiet life in Italy to be boring and unfulfilling. He began to miss his friends and his career, and he felt a sense of frustration and resentment.

They began to argue and fight, and their love began to turn into bitterness and anger. They both realized that they had made a mistake, and that their relationship was a failure. They decided to return to Russia and try to rebuild their lives.

But when they arrived in Petersburg, they found that they were outcasts. Their secret had been discovered, and they were the subject of gossip and scandal. Their friends and relatives refused to see them, and they were excluded from society.

Anna's husband refused to let her see her son, and she felt a sense of despair that she could not bear. She began to use opium to escape from her pain, and she became increasingly unstable and depressed.
Vronsky tried to help her, but he was also struggling. He could not find work, and he felt a sense of failure and humiliation. He began to drink heavily, and he became increasingly distant and cold.
One day, Anna could bear it no longer. She went to the station and threw herself under a train. She died instantly, and her death was a shock to everyone who knew her.

Vronsky was devastated. He felt a sense of guilt and remorse that he could not shake off. He left Russia and went to fight in the war in the Balkans, hoping to find a way to end his life. He was a man who had lost everything, and he had nothing to live for.

The story of Anna Karenina is a tragic one, but it is also a story of love, passion, and the consequences of our actions. It is a story that still resonates today, and it is a reminder of the power of the human heart.

The whole of that day Anna spent at home, that's to say at the Oblonskys', and received no one, though some of her acquaintances had already heard of her arrival, and came to call the same day. Anna spent the whole morning with Dolly and the children. She merely sent a brief note to her brother to tell him that he must not fail to dine at home. "Come, God is merciful," she wrote.

Oblonsky did dine at home: the conversation was general, and his wife, speaking to him, addressed him as "Stiva," as she had not done before. In the relations of the husband and wife the same estrangement still remained, but there was no talk now of separation, and Stepan Arkadyevitch saw the possibility of explanation and reconciliation.

Immediately after dinner Kitty came in. She knew Anna Arkadyevna, but only very slightly, and she came now to her friend's with some trepidation, at the thought of the ordeal she had to face. Kitty felt that Anna Arkadyevna would at once see through her, and she was afraid of her. But Anna was not at all what Kitty had expected her to be. Instead of being the formidable, elegant, and grand lady she had imagined, Anna was simply a very sweet, kind, and fascinating woman, who at once put her at her ease.
"I am so glad to see you," said Anna, taking both Kitty's hands in hers. "I have heard so much about you from Stiva."
Kitty felt herself blushing, but she could not help smiling. "And I have heard so much about you," she replied.

They sat down and began to talk. Anna did not ask her any of the questions Kitty had been dreading, but talked to her about her own life in Petersburg, and about her son, Seryozha. Kitty listened with interest, and she felt that she was beginning to like Anna very much. There was a charm and a grace about her that Kitty had never seen in anyone else.
"You must come and see me in Petersburg," said Anna. "Seryozha would be so glad to know you."
"I should like that very much," replied Kitty.

As they talked, Kitty forgot all about her fears. She felt that she could talk to Anna as she could to no one else, and she found herself telling her things she had never told anyone. She spoke of her love for Vronsky, and of her hopes for the future. Anna listened with a sympathetic smile, but Kitty noticed a strange look in her eyes, a look of sadness and pity.
"He is very handsome, is he not?" said Anna, when Kitty had finished.
"Yes, very," replied Kitty, her face glowing.
"And he is very rich and well-connected," continued Anna. "It is a very good match for you, Kitty."
Kitty felt a sudden pang of doubt. "Do you think so?" she asked.
"Of course," replied Anna. "But you must be careful, Kitty. Love is a very dangerous thing."
"Dangerous? How?" asked Kitty.
"It can make you do things you would never have thought possible," said Anna, her voice dropping to a whisper. "It can make you forget your duty, your family, everything."
Kitty looked at her in surprise. "But surely love is a good thing?" she said.
"Yes, it is a good thing," said Anna, "but it can also be a very terrible thing. You are young, Kitty, and you do not know the world. But I know it, and I tell you, you must be careful."

They were interrupted by the arrival of other guests, and the conversation became general. Kitty stayed for a while, but she felt that she could not stay any longer. She said goodbye to Anna and went home, her mind full of what Anna had said. She felt a sense of unease and foreboding that she could not explain.

The next day was the day of the ball at the Governor's house. Kitty was very excited, and she spent the whole day preparing for it. She wanted to look her best, for she knew that Vronsky would be there. She wore a dress of pale pink silk, with a wreath of roses in her hair. When she looked in the glass, she felt that she had never looked so beautiful.

At the ball, Kitty was the center of attention. She danced with many of the young men, but she was always looking for Vronsky. At last she saw him, and her heart gave a leap of joy. He was standing with a group of officers, and he looked more handsome than ever.
He came up to her and asked her for a dance. As they danced, Kitty felt that she was in heaven. She felt that Vronsky loved her, and that they would be happy together forever. But then she saw Anna.

Anna was wearing a dress of black velvet, which set off her pale skin and dark hair. She looked more beautiful than Kitty had ever seen her. She was surrounded by a crowd of admirers, and she seemed to be enjoying herself. But when her eyes met Vronsky's, Kitty saw that look again-the look of recognition and understanding.

Vronsky seemed to forget all about Kitty. He could not take his eyes off Anna, and he seemed to be drawn to her by a force he could not resist. Kitty saw them dancing together, and she felt a sense of despair and jealousy. She realized that Vronsky did not love her, but Anna.

She went home in a state of misery. She felt that her life was over, and that she had nothing to live for. She thought of what Anna had said about love being a dangerous thing, and she realized that Anna had been right. She had given her heart to a man who did not want it, and she had been betrayed.
In the morning, she told her mother that she was ill and could not go out. She spent the whole day in her room, crying and thinking of Vronsky. She felt that she could never be happy again.
Dolly came out of her room to the tea of the grown-up people. Stepan Arkadyevitch did not come out. He must have left his wife's room by the other door.
"I am afraid you'll be cold upstairs," observed Dolly, addressing Anna; "I want to move you downstairs, and we shall be nearer."
"Oh, please, don't trouble about me," answered Anna, looking intently into Dolly's face, trying to make out whether there had been a reconciliation or not.
"It will be lighter for you here," answered her sister-in-law.
"I assure you that I sleep everywhere, and always like a marmot."
"What's the matter?" asked Stepan Arkadyevitch, coming out of his room and addressing his wife.

From his tone both Kitty and Anna knew that a reconciliation had taken place. Dolly was carrying a cup of tea. With a rapid movement she turned to him and smiled. It was the first time she had smiled at him for days.
"I am telling Anna that she would be better downstairs," she said.

Stepan Arkadyevitch's face shone with satisfaction. He felt that the heavy burden that had been weighing on him was lifted. He was forgiven. He looked at Anna with gratitude, knowing it was her doing.
"Well, then, we'll move her," he said. "It's a capital idea."

The atmosphere in the house had completely changed. The constraint and gloom had vanished, replaced by a tentative, quiet cheerfulness. Even the servants felt the difference and moved about more briskly. Anna, observing them, felt a pang of mingled joy for them and a strange, lonely sadness for herself. She had mended their home, but her own thoughts were drifting further and further toward a world where such simple domestic peace felt impossible.

Kitty, however, was still silent. She sat in the corner, her eyes fixed on her tea, her mind replaying every second of the ball. The reconciliation of her sister and brother-in-law, which would have overjoyed her two days ago, now seemed distant and insignificant compared to the storm in her own heart.
"And what are you so quiet about, Kitty?" asked Stepan Arkadyevitch, pinching her cheek. "Thinking of the conquests of last night?"
Kitty forced a smile, but her eyes remained dark. "I am just tired, Stiva," she whispered.

Anna watched her. She knew that look. She saw in Kitty the person she herself had once been-vulnerable, expectant, and standing on the threshold of a great, crushing disappointment. She felt an impulse to go to her, to warn her, but she knew that words were useless against the first fever of love.
"You must rest, then," Anna said gently. "The first balls are always the most exhausting. They demand so much of the spirit."

Later that evening, when the house was quiet, Anna sat by the window in her new room downstairs. She thought of her husband in Petersburg, of his dry, regular habits and his career. She thought of Seryozha, and her heart ached with a sudden, sharp longing. But through all these thoughts, the face of Vronsky kept appearing-the way he had looked at her on the platform, and the way he had ignored everyone else in the ballroom just to watch her breathe.

She realized then that she was not just a visitor in Moscow; she was a refugee from a life that had suddenly become unbearable. The reconciliation she had brought to Dolly and Stiva felt like a gift she could no longer give herself.

The ball was only just beginning as Kitty and her mother walked up the great staircase, flooded with light, and lined with flowers and footmen in powder and red coats. From the rooms came a constant, steady hum, as from a hive, and the rustle of movement; and while on the landing between trees they gave last touches to their hair and dresses before the mirror, they heard from the ballroom the careful, distinct notes of the fiddles of the orchestra beginning the first waltz.
